extra.reasoning:
The property G(opted_out -> not sent) is an inductive invariant preserved by every transition, so it holds on all reachable states (well within the 7-event bound).

Key observations on the two relevant variables:
- sent := true occurs ONLY in t2 (send_attempt, guard: not opted_out). Every other transition that touches sent sets it to false: t1, t3-t14, and t15 all assign sent := false.
- opted_out := true occurs ONLY in t1 (optout), and t1 simultaneously sets sent := false.

Inductive argument:
- Initial state: opted_out=false, sent=false. Implication holds (antecedent false).
- t1 (optout): sets opted_out=true but also sent=false. Result has not sent, so opted_out -> not sent holds.
- t2 (send_attempt, not opted_out): sets sent=true only while opted_out=false. opted_out remains false, so the implication is vacuously satisfied.
- t15 (send_attempt, opted_out): sets sent=false. Holds.
- t3-t14 (key presses): all set sent=false and never change opted_out. Holds.

Thus no transition can produce a state with opted_out=true AND sent=true. To have sent=true, the most recent write to sent must be t2 (requiring opted_out=false then); to subsequently make opted_out=true requires firing t1, which clears sent. There is no path that sets opted_out true without clearing sent, and no path that sets sent true while opted_out is true. The invariant is preserved on every reachable execution, so the safety property holds.
